worth_it_explanation: Made-for-advertising (MFA) is a label buyers apply to inventory built mainly to show ads rather than serve readers, and demand-side platforms exclude it through filters and inclusion lists. Inventory that reads as MFA loses access to that demand, so the audit protects revenue that depends on staying off those exclusion lists. The signals buyers use are observable without any platform data, namely ad density, content depth, traffic sourcing, and the length of the resold supply path. The fixes are within the publisher's control, so a clean result here keeps demand flowing rather than recovering it after the fact.
intro: Made-for-advertising describes sites built mainly to display ads rather than serve readers, and buyers increasingly exclude that inventory from their campaigns. A publisher whose pages carry MFA characteristics, or whose inventory is resold through a long chain of intermediaries, can lose demand and trust without an obvious cause. These checks look at the page-level signals buyers read and the supply-path length they associate with low-quality supply, so the auditor can flag the traits that put a publisher's Google Ad Manager (GAM) inventory at risk.

audit_checks.title: Inventory shows made-for-advertising characteristics
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inventory that carries made-for-advertising traits gets filtered out by demand-side exclusion lists, so the affected pages lose access to bidders even when the traffic is real. The revenue drop reads as soft demand rather than a quality flag, so the cause goes untraced while the inventory keeps clearing at a lower rate.

educate.base:
Made-for-advertising (MFA) is the label buyers apply to inventory that exists mainly to show ads rather than serve readers. There is no single switch that marks a page as MFA. Buyers read a cluster of signals and exclude the inventory that trips enough of them, which removes that inventory from the campaigns running through their demand-side platform.

The traits buyers weigh are the same ones a publisher can observe directly. The clearest is ad density, meaning how much of the page is ads relative to content. Google's publisher policies prohibit Google-served ads on screens where advertising or paid promotional material exceeds the publisher content, and a page that crowds ads above and around thin content reads as MFA to buyers as well. The second trait is content depth. Pages with little original content, or content that is auto-generated or copied without curation, fall under Google's low-value content policy and signal MFA to demand. The third is traffic sourcing, meaning a large share of paid or arbitraged visits arriving on pages built to recirculate users through more ad slots.

These traits compound. A page with a high ad-to-content ratio, thin or auto-generated text, and a heavy share of sourced traffic reads as MFA on several axes at once, and buyers prune it from their inventory lists. The pruning is invisible from the publisher's side. The pages still serve ads, just to fewer bidders and at a lower clearing price.

A few signals point to inventory that buyers may read as made-for-advertising:
  • An ad-to-content ratio where ads occupy as much of the viewport as the content, or more
  • Thin, auto-generated, or copied content with no added commentary or curation
  • A layout that pushes the reader through additional ad-laden pages, such as slideshows or infinite recirculation
  • A large share of paid or sourced traffic relative to organic and direct visits
You may want to assess each page template against these signals rather than the site as a whole since a single ad-heavy template can pull demand away even when the rest of the inventory reads as content-first.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the article template is where ad density and content depth are judged together, so a long-form article surrounded by a reasonable number of ad slots reads very differently from a 200-word post wrapped in the same slots. You may want to compare your highest-traffic templates against the publisher content policy and confirm the content carries the page rather than the ad slots.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product roundups and buying guides can drift toward made-for-advertising when the editorial thins to a few lines between affiliate blocks and display ads. You may want to confirm each guide adds original commentary and curation rather than serving as a frame for the ad and affiliate units around it.

learn_more.source_title: More ads or paid promotional material than publisher-content

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's highest-traffic page templates for ad density, content depth, and traffic sourcing, judged against the Google Publisher Policies.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open a representative content page and launch the Google Publisher Console (append '?googfc' to the URL or use the bookmarklet).
List the ad slots the page requests and where they sit relative to the content, noting any slot that loads above the fold ahead of the article body.
Estimate the share of the initial viewport taken by ads versus content, and flag any template where ads occupy as much of the view as the content or more.

manual_page_review.label: Manual page review
steps: Open the three or four highest-traffic page templates and read each as a visitor would.
Note whether the content is original and substantive, or thin, auto-generated, or copied without added commentary, against the low-value content policy.
Note any layout that recirculates the reader through extra ad-laden pages, such as a slideshow split across many screens.
Where analytics are available to the auditor, record the share of traffic arriving from paid or sourced channels versus organic and direct.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per high-traffic template, the observed ad-to-content ratio, the content quality, the layout pattern, and the traffic-sourcing mix. A template whose content carries the page, whose ads stay within the publisher content policy, and whose traffic is mostly organic or direct passes. A template with ads occupying as much of the view as the content or more, thin or auto-generated content, a recirculation layout, or a heavy share of sourced traffic is a fail recorded with the template name and the signals that tripped.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the ad-to-content ratio, content quality, layout pattern, and traffic-sourcing mix for each high-traffic template above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several of {{site_name}}'s high-traffic templates show made-for-advertising (MFA) characteristics, namely a high ad-to-content ratio, thin content, and a heavy share of sourced traffic. Buyers read those signals and exclude the inventory through their demand-side filters, so the affected pages reach fewer bidders and clear at a lower price even though the traffic is real. {{voice_recommend}} reducing the ad density on the flagged templates so the content carries the page, strengthening thin or auto-generated content with original commentary and curation, and reviewing the paid-traffic sources that feed those templates. After the changes, recheck each template against the Google Publisher Policies to confirm the content reads as the focal point rather than a frame for the ads.

audit_checks.title: Resold paths inflate the supply chain and raise MFA-adjacent risk
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Long reseller chains lengthen the supply path between the publisher and the buyer, and buyers associate that length with low-quality and made-for-advertising supply, so they prune the longer paths. A publisher reselling through many intermediaries can be caught in that pruning and lose demand that a shorter, verified path would have kept.

educate.base:
Every impression a buyer wins travels a supply path, meaning the chain of intermediaries between the publisher that owns the inventory and the buyer that bids on it. The sellers.json and SupplyChain object specifications from IAB Tech Lab let a buyer see every entity in that chain and how each one is declared, so the buyer can judge how direct the path is and whether each hop is authorized.

Buyers favor short, verified paths. A long chain of resellers, or a path with hops that are not authorized in the relevant sellers.json files, lengthens the supply path and signals low-quality or made-for-advertising (MFA) supply since arbitraged and resold inventory passes through more intermediaries before it reaches the buyer. Buyers prune those longer paths in favor of the publisher's most direct route, so inventory that is mostly available through deep reseller chains loses demand it would keep on a shorter path.

In Google Ad Manager (GAM), the resold portion of a publisher's path runs through Multiple Customer Management (MCM), which is how a parent network represents or manages a child publisher's inventory. Each MCM relationship adds a declared hop to the chain. A child publisher delegated to several parents, or a parent representing inventory it cannot fully vouch for, lengthens the path and adds hops a buyer has to trust. Keeping the resold path short, and confirming every hop is a real, authorized relationship declared in sellers.json, keeps the publisher on the direct paths buyers prefer.

A few signals point to a resold path that may read as inflated:
  • A long chain of resellers between the publisher and the buyer in the SupplyChain object
  • MCM child relationships delegated to more parent networks than the inventory volume warrants
  • Hops in the path that are not declared, or not marked as authorized, in the matching sellers.json file
You may want to map the publisher's declared sellers against the relationships they can actually account for since a hop no one can vouch for is the kind buyers prune first.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that resells some inventory through partners, the direct path and the resold path can clear at very different rates, so a buyer reaching the inventory through a four-hop reseller chain may bid lower or not at all. You may want to confirm each reseller relationship is a real, authorized partner declared in sellers.json, and retire the hops that no longer carry meaningful volume.

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's reseller depth in sellers.json and the Multiple Customer Management (MCM) child relationships in Google Ad Manager, then judge how short and how verified the resold path is.

sellers_json_file.label: sellers.json file
steps: Open the sellers.json file of the network monetizing the inventory (e.g., google.com/sellers.json for Google Ad Manager) and locate the publisher's seller entries.
Note whether each entry is declared as a publisher, an intermediary, or both, and whether the relationship is one a buyer can trace to a real partner.
Where the inventory is resold, follow the chain of intermediaries and count the hops between the publisher and the point where a buyer would bid.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: In Google Ad Manager, open the MCM area under account access and review the child or parent relationships in place.
List each delegation, note whether it is Manage Account or Manage Inventory, and confirm it maps to a real, authorized partner.
Flag any relationship that adds a hop the publisher cannot account for, or that delegates more parents than the inventory volume warrants.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the count of reseller hops in the supply path, the MCM relationships in place, and whether each hop is declared and authorized in the matching sellers.json file. A short path where every hop maps to a real, authorized relationship passes. A long reseller chain, MCM delegations that exceed what the inventory warrants, or hops that are undeclared or unauthorized is a fail recorded with the path and the hops at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reseller hop count, the MCM relationships, and the sellers.json authorization status for each path above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s inventory reaches buyers through a long reseller chain, with hops that are not all declared or authorized in the matching sellers.json files. Buyers associate a long supply path with low-quality and made-for-advertising supply, so they prune those paths in favor of the publisher's direct route, which costs {{site_name}} demand a shorter path would keep. {{voice_recommend}} shortening the resold path by retiring Multiple Customer Management (MCM) relationships that no longer carry meaningful volume, and confirming every remaining hop is a real, authorized partner declared in sellers.json. After the cleanup, recheck the SupplyChain object to confirm the path is short and every hop is verified.
